335 (1) Subject to subsection (1.1), every one who, without the consent of the owner, takes a motor vehicle or vessel with intent to drive, use, navigate or operate it or cause it to be driven, used, navigated or operated, or is an occupant of a motor vehicle or vessel knowing that it was taken without the consent of the owner, is guilty of an offence punishable on summary conviction.
(1.1) Exception — Subsection (1) does not apply to an occupant of a motor vehicle or vessel who, on becoming aware that it was taken without the consent of the owner, attempted to leave the motor vehicle or vessel, to the extent that it was feasible to do so, or actually left the motor vehicle or vessel.
(2) Definition of vessel — For the purposes of subsection (1), vessel has the same meaning as in section 320.11.
offences.shortForm: Taking motor vehicle or vessel or fou...
offences.maxPenalty: Summary: an offence punishable on summary conviction (no specific term stated in the section).

offences.id: Criminal Code:342:Theft, forgery, etc., of credit card
offences.title: Theft, forgery, etc., of credit card
offences.bodyText:
342 (1) Every person who
(a) steals a credit card,
(b) forges or falsifies a credit card,
(c) possesses, uses or traffics in a credit card or a forged or falsified credit card, knowing that it was obtained, made or altered
(i) by the commission in Canada of an offence, or
(ii) by an act or omission anywhere that, if it had occurred in Canada, would have constituted an offence, or
(d) uses a credit card knowing that it has been revoked or cancelled,
is guilty of
(e) an indictable offence and is liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years, or
(f) an offence punishable on summary conviction.
offences.shortForm: Theft, forgery, etc., of credit card
offences.maxPenalty: Indictable: imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years; Summary: an offence punishable on summary conviction.

offences.title: Unauthorized use of computer
offences.maxPenalty: Indictable: imprisonment for a term of not more than 10 years; Summary: an offence punishable on summary conviction.

offences.title: Robbery
offences.bodyText: Every one commits robbery who (a) steals, and for the purpose of extorting whatever is stolen or to prevent or overcome resistance to the stealing, uses violence or threats of violence to a person or property; (b) steals from any person and, at the time he steals or immediately before or immediately thereafter, wounds, beats, strikes or uses any personal violence to that person; (c) assaults any person with intent to steal from him; or (d) steals from any person while armed with an offensive weapon or imitation thereof.

offences.title: Robbery
offences.bodyText: Every person who commits robbery is guilty of an indictable offence and liable (a) if a restricted firearm or prohibited firearm is used in the commission of the offence or if any firearm is used in the commission of the offence and the offence is committed for the benefit of, at the direction of, or in association with, a criminal organization, to imprisonment for life and to a minimum punishment of imprisonment for a term of (i) in the case of a first offence, five years, and (ii) in the case of a second or subsequent offence, seven years; ... (b) in any other case, to imprisonment for life.
offences.maxPenalty: life
offences.minPenalty: where a restricted/prohibited firearm is used, or any firearm is used for a criminal organization: 5 years (first offence), 7 years (second or subsequent offence); otherwise none

offences.title: Extortion
offences.bodyText: Every one commits extortion who, without reasonable justification or excuse and with intent to obtain anything, by threats, accusations, menaces or violence induces or attempts to induce any person, whether or not he is the person threatened, accused or menaced or to whom violence is shown, to do anything or cause anything to be done. Every person who commits extortion is guilty of an indictable offence and liable ... in any other case, to imprisonment for life.
offences.maxPenalty: life
offences.minPenalty: where a restricted/prohibited firearm is used, or any firearm is used for a criminal organization: 5 years (first offence), 7 years (second or subsequent offence); otherwise none

offences.id: Criminal Code:348:Breaking and entering with intent, committing offence or breaking out
offences.title: Breaking and entering with intent, committing offence or breaking out
offences.bodyText: Every one who (a) breaks and enters a place with intent to commit an indictable offence therein, (b) breaks and enters a place and commits an indictable offence therein, or (c) breaks out of a place after committing/entering with intent, is guilty (d) if the offence is committed in relation to a dwelling-house, of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for life, and (e) if the offence is committed in relation to a place other than a dwelling-house, of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years or of an offence punishable on summary conviction.
offences.shortForm: Breaking and entering with intent, co...
offences.maxPenalty: life (dwelling-house); 10 years (place other than a dwelling-house)

offences.title: Being unlawfully in dwelling-house
offences.bodyText: Every person who, without lawful excuse, enters or is in a dwelling-house with intent to commit an indictable offence in it is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term of not more than 10 years or of an offence punishable on summary conviction.
offences.maxPenalty: 10 years

offences.id: Criminal Code:351:Possession of break-in instrument (351(1)); Disguise with intent (351(2))
offences.title: Possession of break-in instrument (351(1)); Disguise with intent (351(2))
offences.bodyText: (1) Every person who, without lawful excuse, has in their possession any instrument suitable for the purpose of breaking into any place, motor vehicle, vault or safe knowing that the instrument has been used or is intended to be used for that purpose, (a) is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years; or (b) is guilty of an offence punishable on summary conviction. (2) Every person who, with intent to commit an indictable offence, has their face masked or coloured or is otherwise disguised is guilty of (a) an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term of not more than 10 years; or (b) an offence punishable on summary conviction.
offences.maxPenalty: 10 years

offences.id: Criminal Code:354:Possession of property obtained by crime
offences.title: Possession of property obtained by crime
offences.bodyText: Every one commits an offence who has in his possession any property or thing or any proceeds of any property or thing knowing that all or part of the property or thing or of the proceeds was obtained by or derived directly or indirectly from (a) the commission in Canada of an offence punishable by indictment; or (b) an act or omission anywhere that, if it had occurred in Canada, would have constituted an offence punishable by indictment. (Punishment set out in s.355.)
offences.shortForm: Possession of property obtained by crime
offences.maxPenalty: Testamentary instrument or value over $5,000: indictable, imprisonment up to 10 years, or summary conviction (s.355(a)). Value not more than $5,000: indictable, imprisonment not exceeding 2 years, or summary conviction (s.355(b)).

offences.id: Criminal Code:355.2:Trafficking in property obtained by crime
offences.title: Trafficking in property obtained by crime
offences.bodyText: Everyone commits an offence who traffics in any property or any thing or any proceeds of any property or thing knowing that all or part of the property, thing or proceeds was obtained by or derived directly or indirectly from (a) the commission in Canada of an offence punishable by indictment; or (b) an act or omission anywhere that, if it had occurred in Canada, would have constituted an offence punishable by indictment. (Punishment set out in s.355.5.)
offences.shortForm: Trafficking in property obtained by c...
offences.maxPenalty: Value over $5,000: indictable, imprisonment up to 14 years (s.355.5(a)). Value not more than $5,000: indictable, imprisonment up to 5 years, or summary conviction (s.355.5(b)).

offences.id: Criminal Code:362:False pretence or false statement
offences.title: False pretence or false statement
offences.bodyText: Every one commits an offence who (a) by a false pretence, whether directly or through the medium of a contract obtained by a false pretence, obtains anything in respect of which the offence of theft may be committed or causes it to be delivered to another person; (b) obtains credit by a false pretence or by fraud; ...
offences.maxPenalty: Offence under s.362(1)(a): testamentary or property obtained over $5,000: indictable up to 10 years, or summary; value not more than $5,000: indictable not exceeding 2 years, or summary (s.362(2)). Offence under s.362(1)(b),(c),(d): indictable up to 10 years, or summary (s.362(3)).

offences.title: Forgery
offences.bodyText: Every one commits forgery who makes a false document, knowing it to be false, with intent (a) that it should in any way be used or acted on as genuine, to the prejudice of any one whether within Canada or not; or (b) that a person should be induced, by the belief that it is genuine, to do or to refrain from doing anything, whether within Canada or not.
offences.maxPenalty: Punishment set out in s.367: indictable, imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or summary conviction.

offences.title: Punishment for forgery
offences.bodyText: Every one who commits forgery (a) is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years; or (b) is guilty of an offence punishable on summary conviction.
offences.maxPenalty: Indictable, imprisonment not exceeding 10 years; or summary conviction.

offences.id: Criminal Code:368:Use, trafficking or possession of forged document
offences.title: Use, trafficking or possession of forged document
offences.bodyText: Everyone commits an offence who, knowing or believing that a document is forged, (a) uses, deals with or acts on it as if it were genuine; (b) causes or attempts to cause any person to use, deal with or act on it as if it were genuine; (c) transfers, sells or offers to sell it or makes it available, to any person, knowing that or being reckless as to whether an offence will be committed under paragraph (a) or (b); or (d) possesses it with intent to commit an offence under any of paragraphs (a) to (c).
offences.shortForm: Use, trafficking or possession of for...
offences.maxPenalty: Punishment set out in s.368(1.1): indictable, imprisonment of not more than 10 years; or summary conviction.

offences.title: Fraud
offences.bodyText: Every one who, by deceit, falsehood or other fraudulent means, whether or not it is a false pretence within the meaning of this Act, defrauds the public or any person, whether ascertained or not, of any property, money or valuable security or any service ... (a) is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to a term of imprisonment not exceeding fourteen years, where the subject-matter of the offence is a testamentary instrument or the value of the subject-matter of the offence exceeds five thousand dollars.
offences.maxPenalty: Testamentary instrument or value of subject-matter over $5,000: indictable, imprisonment not exceeding 14 years (s.380(1)(a)). Value not exceeding $5,000: indictable not exceeding 2 years, or summary conviction (s.380(1)(b)).
offences.minPenalty: Minimum 2 years' imprisonment on conviction on indictment where the total value of the subject-matter of the offences exceeds one million dollars (s.380(1.1)).

offences.title: Mischief
offences.bodyText: Every one who commits mischief that causes actual danger to life is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for life (subsection (2)); mischief in relation to a testamentary instrument or property whose value exceeds five thousand dollars is punishable by indictment up to ten years or on summary conviction (subsection (3)).
offences.maxPenalty: Varies by subsection: (2) mischief causing actual danger to life — indictable, imprisonment for life; (3) property that is a testamentary instrument or whose value exceeds $5,000 — indictable up to 10 years or summary; (4) other property — indictable up to 2 years or summary; (4.1) religious/educational property motivated by bias/prejudice/hate — indictable up to 10 years or summary; (4.11) war memorials — indictable up to 10 years / summary up to 2 years less a day; (4.2) cultural property — indictable up to 10 years or summary; (5) computer data — indictable up to 10 years or summary; (5.1) act likely to cause danger to life or mischief — indictable up to 5 years or summary.
offences.minPenalty: Only for mischief relating to war memorials (4.11): first offence a fine of not less than $1,000; second offence imprisonment for not less than 14 days; each subsequent offence imprisonment for not less than 30 days. No minimum for the other mischief subsections.

offences.id: Criminal Code:433:Arson — disregard for human life
offences.title: Arson — disregard for human life
offences.bodyText: Every person who intentionally or recklessly causes damage by fire or explosion to property, whether or not that person owns the property, is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for life where (a) the person knows that or is reckless with respect to whether the property is inhabited or occupied; or (b) the fire or explosion causes bodily harm to another person.
offences.maxPenalty: Imprisonment for life

offences.title: Arson — damage to property
offences.bodyText: Every person who intentionally or recklessly causes damage by fire or explosion to property that is not wholly owned by that person is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding fourteen years.
offences.maxPenalty: Imprisonment for a term not exceeding fourteen years

offences.title: Arson for fraudulent purpose
offences.bodyText: Every person who, with intent to defraud any other person, causes damage by fire or explosion to property, whether or not that person owns, in whole or in part, the property, is guilty of (a) an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term of not more than 10 years; or (b) an offence punishable on summary conviction.
offences.maxPenalty: On indictment, imprisonment for a term of not more than 10 years; or an offence punishable on summary conviction

offences.title: Making (counterfeit money)
offences.bodyText: Every one who makes or begins to make counterfeit money is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding fourteen years.
offences.maxPenalty: Imprisonment for a term not exceeding fourteen years

offences.id: Criminal Code:450:Possession, etc., of counterfeit money
offences.title: Possession, etc., of counterfeit money
offences.bodyText: Every person is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term of not more than 14 years who, without lawful justification or excuse, (a) buys, receives or offers to buy or receive counterfeit money; (b) has their custody or possession counterfeit money; or (c) introduces counterfeit money into Canada.
offences.maxPenalty: Imprisonment for a term of not more than 14 years

offences.id: Criminal Code:463:Attempts, accessories after the fact
offences.title: Attempts, accessories after the fact
offences.bodyText: 463 Except where otherwise expressly provided by law, the following provisions apply in respect of persons who attempt to commit or are accessories after the fact to the commission of offences: (a) every one who attempts to commit or is an accessory after the fact to an indictable offence for which a person is liable to imprisonment for life is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding fourteen years; (b) where the maximum is fourteen years or less, liable to one-half of the longest term; (c) offences punishable on summary conviction, summary; (d) hybrid offences, indictable (one-half) or summary.
offences.maxPenalty: Mirrors the completed offence: attempt/accessory to an offence punishable by life = max 14 years (indictable); to an indictable offence of 14 years or less = one-half of the longest term; to a summary offence = summary; hybrid follows the mode elected.

offences.id: Criminal Code:464:Counselling offence that is not committed
offences.title: Counselling offence that is not committed
offences.bodyText: 464 Except where otherwise expressly provided by law, the following provisions apply in respect of persons who counsel other persons to commit offences: (a) every one who counsels another person to commit an indictable offence is, if the offence is not committed, guilty of an indictable offence and liable to the same punishment to which a person who attempts to commit that offence is liable; and (b) every one who counsels another person to commit an offence punishable on summary conviction is, if the offence is not committed, guilty of an offence punishable on summary conviction.
offences.shortForm: Counselling offence that is not commi...
offences.maxPenalty: Same punishment as attempting the counselled offence: counselling an indictable offence not committed = indictable (same liability as an attempt); counselling a summary offence not committed = summary.

offences.title: Conspiracy
offences.bodyText: 465 (1) Except where otherwise expressly provided by law, the following provisions apply in respect of conspiracy: (a) every one who conspires with any one to commit murder or to cause another person to be murdered, whether in Canada or not, is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to a maximum term of imprisonment for life; (b) every one who conspires to prosecute a person for an alleged offence, knowing that they did not commit it, is liable to imprisonment (up to 10 years, or up to 14 years where the alleged offence would carry a longer term); (c) every one who conspires to commit an indictable offence not in (a) or (b) is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to the same punishment as that to which an accused who is guilty of that offence would be liable; and (d) every one who conspires to commit a summary offence is guilty of an offence punishable on summary conviction.
offences.maxPenalty: Conspiracy to commit murder or to cause another person to be murdered: indictable, imprisonment for life. Conspiracy to commit any other indictable offence: same punishment as if the accused had committed that offence. Conspiracy to falsely prosecute: up to 10 years (or 14 years if the alleged offence carries a longer term). Conspiracy to commit a summary offence: summary.

offences.id: Criminal Code:733.1:Failure to comply with probation order
offences.title: Failure to comply with probation order
offences.bodyText: 733.1 (1) An offender who is bound by a probation order and who, without reasonable excuse, fails or refuses to comply with that order is guilty of (a) an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term of not more than four years; or (b) an offence punishable on summary conviction.
offences.shortForm: Failure to comply with probation order
offences.maxPenalty: Indictable: 4 years; Summary: general summary conviction penalty (2 years less a day)

offences.title: Breach of recognizance (peace bond)
offences.bodyText: 811 A person bound by a recognizance under any of sections 83.3 and 810 to 810.2 who commits a breach of the recognizance is guilty of (a) an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term of not more than four years; or (b) an offence punishable on summary conviction.
offences.maxPenalty: Indictable: 4 years; Summary: general summary conviction penalty (2 years less a day)

caseLaw.summary: Consent does not provide a defence to assault where the accused both intended and caused serious bodily harm in a consensual fist fight; the common law limits the effect of consent to protect against the harm being inflicted.

There is no defence of implied consent to sexual assault in Canadian law; consent means the complainant's subjective, contemporaneous agreement to each sexual act, and a belief in consent must be grounded in communicated words or conduct, not the accused's assumptions.

Consent to sexual activity must be present at the time the activity occurs, so a person cannot give advance consent to sexual acts performed while they are unconscious; the Code requires an ongoing, conscious capacity to agree.

Sexual assault is an assault committed in circumstances of a sexual nature such that the victim's sexual integrity is violated, assessed objectively; the act need not involve contact with a specific body part to qualify.

An accused relying on an honest but mistaken belief in communicated consent must show they took reasonable steps to ascertain consent, and prior sexual activity cannot be used to infer consent to the acts charged.

Failing to disclose HIV status can vitiate consent and support an aggravated sexual assault charge, but only where there is a realistic possibility of transmission; a low viral load combined with condom use may negate that possibility.

Fraud that vitiates consent to sexual contact requires dishonesty causing a deprivation in the form of a significant risk of serious bodily harm, establishing that non-disclosure of a serious health risk can convert consensual contact into assault.

Unlawfully causing bodily harm requires an underlying unlawful act that is objectively dangerous, plus objective foreseeability of the risk of bodily harm; the Crown need not prove the accused foresaw the specific harm caused.

A murder conviction requires proof of subjective foresight of death; provisions allowing conviction for murder on a lesser mental state violate the principles of fundamental justice under the Charter.

For homicide, the accused's conduct must be a significant contributing cause of death; the standard of causation is expressed as a contribution beyond something trivial or minor rather than requiring the sole or main cause.

Manslaughter based on an unlawful act requires only objective foreseeability of the risk of bodily harm that is neither trivial nor transitory, not foreseeability of death; the standard is that of the reasonable person.

An accused may be convicted of manslaughter where the unlawful act was a contributing cause of death outside the de minimis range, even if the victim's pre-existing vulnerability contributed to the fatal result.

Uttering threats is made out where the words, viewed objectively in context, convey a threat of serious bodily harm and were spoken with the intent to intimidate or be taken seriously; it is not necessary that the accused intended to carry out the threat.

The threat need not be communicated to its target and no intent to actually harm is required; the offence targets words meant to intimidate or instill fear, assessed objectively from the standpoint of a reasonable listener aware of the circumstances.

The child pornography provisions are largely constitutional, but the Court read in narrow exceptions for privately created expressive material and private recordings of lawful sexual activity involving the accused, balancing child protection against expression and privacy.

The mens rea of fraud is established by subjective knowledge of the prohibited act (a dishonest deprivation) and knowledge that it could cause deprivation; a sincere belief that the scheme would ultimately succeed or cause no loss is not a defence.

Dangerous driving requires a marked departure from the standard of care of a reasonable driver; a momentary lapse of attention, without more, is generally insufficient to establish the criminal fault required.

To convict of dangerous driving the Crown must prove both that the driving was objectively dangerous and that it constituted a marked departure from the reasonable standard; a mere departure, or the fact of a serious accident, is not enough.

Care or control of a motor vehicle while impaired requires a realistic risk of danger to persons or property arising from the accused's use of the vehicle or its equipment; occupying an impaired person's seat may create that risk even without an intent to drive.

The mandatory minimum sentences then attached to the prohibited or restricted firearm possession offence were struck down as cruel and unusual punishment because reasonably foreseeable applications could impose grossly disproportionate sentences on less culpable offenders.

The mandatory minimum for intentionally discharging a firearm at a place was found unconstitutional because the provision could capture conduct, such as firing certain air-powered guns at a dwelling, for which a four-year minimum would be grossly disproportionate.

Retribution and proportionality are legitimate sentencing principles, and appellate courts owe deference to a trial judge's sentence absent an error in principle or a demonstrably unfit result; the Court affirmed a lengthy fixed-term sentence for grave offences.

In assessing self-defence, the trier of fact must weigh the person's role in the incident as one of the statutory factors bearing on whether the act was reasonable in the circumstances; conduct throughout the confrontation, not just at the fatal moment, is relevant.
